problems.solution: Prune long stems every 2 months; cuttings root in water.
Move closer to a window — bright indirect light intensifies color.
Touch the soil: water only when the top 2 cm are dry.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: if dry, water until it drains.

singonio.placementRecommended: 1-2 m from a window with bright indirect light — pink cultivars need more light.
placementAlternatives: Bright room with sheer curtain
High shelf where it can trail
Near an east-facing window
singonio.placementAvoid: Direct sun (fades color) and dark corners (loses variegation).
singonio.whyRationale: Central American hemi-epiphytic aroid — in habitat it climbs trees toward filtered light. Leaf shape shifts with height and available light (heteroblasty).

aglaonema.name: Chinese Evergreen
aglaonema.tip: One of the few plants that thrives in low light. Nearly indestructible if not overwatered.
aglaonema.description: Aglaonema is a shade-tolerant Asian aroid. Modern cultivars feature silver, red, or green leaves with decorative patterns. Excellent for offices and corners far from windows.

problems.solution: Let the soil dry well between waterings; check pot drainage.
Accept the slow pace or move to slightly brighter indirect light.
Use water rested for 24h and mist the surrounding air occasionally.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm: if dry, water sparingly; aglaonema prefers less than more.

aglaonema.placementRecommended: Low-light corners — hallways, offices, bedrooms without a direct window.
placementAlternatives: Living room with medium indirect light
Room with fluorescent lighting
Far from windows (deep interior)
aglaonema.placementAvoid: Direct sun (scorches variegated leaves) and cold drafts below 13°C.
aglaonema.whyRationale: Aroid from Asian tropical understory — adapted to minimal forest-floor light. Tolerates low light because its chlorophyll operates efficiently with reduced radiation.

costilla-adan.name: Swiss Cheese Vine
costilla-adan.tip: Smaller cousin of Monstera deliciosa with perforated leaves. Climber ideal for moss pole or hanging basket.
costilla-adan.description: Swiss Cheese Vine (Monstera adansonii) has smaller, more perforated leaves than Monstera deliciosa. A Central American hemi-epiphyte that grows as a climber on a moss pole or trails from a hanging basket. Faster and more compact than its larger cousin.

problems.solution: Move closer to bright indirect light; holes appear with maturity and good light.
Add a moss or coir pole — its aerial roots need grip.
Touch the top 2 cm: water only when dry.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 3 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: if dry, water until it drains.

costilla-adan.placementRecommended: Near a bright indirect window — with a moss pole if you want larger leaves.
placementAlternatives: Hanging basket in bright room
High shelf with stems trailing down
Wall with vertical coir pole
costilla-adan.placementAvoid: Direct sun (scorches the holed leaves) and very dark corners (small leaves with no perforations).
costilla-adan.whyRationale: Central American hemi-epiphytic aroid — distinct from Monstera deliciosa. Climbs understory trees and develops fenestrations to resist winds and share light with lower leaves.

difenbaquia.name: Dumb Cane
difenbaquia.tip: Thick stems with large variegated leaves. Toxic if ingested — keep away from curious pets.
difenbaquia.description: Dumb Cane (Dieffenbachia seguine) has large green leaves with cream or white markings. Grows quickly indoors in medium light. Its sap contains oxalate crystals — handle with gloves when pruning.

problems.symptom: Long bare stem at the base
problems.solution: Prune the top to force branching; cuttings root in water or substrate.
Water at room temperature; touch the soil before watering.
Keep steady moisture (not waterlogged) — dumb cane doesn't tolerate prolonged drought.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 3 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: if dry, water until it drains; use room-temperature water.

difenbaquia.placementRecommended: 1-2 m from a window — medium indirect light, no direct sun on foliage.
placementAlternatives: Living room with diffuse natural light
Bright hallway
Room with bright indirect light
difenbaquia.placementAvoid: Direct sun (scorches variegation) and temperatures below 15°C (leaf drop).
difenbaquia.whyRationale: South American tropical aroid — its stems store water and re-sprout after pruning. The variegation is chlorophyll-free tissue that requires more light to compensate.

begonia-rex.name: Rex Begonia
begonia-rex.tip: Grown for its painted leaves, not its flowers. High humidity, no water on the foliage.
begonia-rex.description: Rex Begonia is a foliage cultivar with asymmetric leaves painted in silver, purple, red, and green. Native to the tropical Himalayas. More demanding than flowering begonias: needs high ambient humidity and water that never touches the leaves.

problems.cause: Water on foliage or dry air
problems.solution: Water the substrate only (never the leaves); use a humidifier.
Move near a bright indirect window — pigments need light to synthesize.
Use a pot with drainage and an airy substrate; touch the soil before watering.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water the substrate (not the leaves) when dry.

begonia-rex.placementRecommended: Bathroom or kitchen with bright indirect light — high natural humidity and no direct sun.
placementAlternatives: Room with a humidifier nearby
Bright shelf behind a sheer curtain
1 m from an east-facing window
begonia-rex.placementAvoid: Direct sun (fades pigments) and dry heater air.
begonia-rex.whyRationale: Begonia cultivar from the Himalayas — anthocyanins and carotenoids in leaves require bright light to express. Without it the leaves lose their decorative pattern.

croton.name: Croton
croton.tip: More light, more intense color. Glossy leaves, almost sculptural.
croton.description: Croton (Codiaeum variegatum) has leathery leaves striped in yellow, orange, red, and green. Native to the tropical Pacific. Sensitive to abrupt changes: drops leaves if moved often. Once settled, leave it where it is.

problems.cause: Location change, draft, or irregular watering
problems.solution: Keep it stable: same spot, same watering, no drafts — it will re-leaf.
Move closer to bright indirect light — color requires light intensity.
Mist the surrounding air; keep watering steady with barely damp substrate.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: if dry, water until it drains; never let it dry out completely.

croton.placementRecommended: Near a bright indirect window or filtered morning sun — color depends on light.
placementAlternatives: 50 cm from an east window
Bright covered porch
Room with filtered morning sun
croton.placementAvoid: Frequent location changes and drafts — croton is stressed by movement.
croton.whyRationale: Native to the tropical Pacific — its anthocyanins and carotenoids need bright light to synthesize. Without enough light the leaves revert to uniform green.

fitonia.name: Nerve Plant
fitonia.tip: Tiny and dramatic. If you see it wilted, water it — it recovers quickly but doesn't forgive repeated drought.
fitonia.description: Nerve Plant (Fittonia albivenis) has small leaves with bold white, pink, or red veins. Native to the Amazon understory. Ideal for terrariums or pots with high ambient humidity — its size makes it perfect for tables and desks.

problems.solution: Water immediately — fittonia recovers quickly but repeated drought kills it.
Reduce watering; touch the soil — top should be barely dry.
Move to a bathroom or cover with a glass dome (terrarium).

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water as soon as it feels dry — never let it wilt.

fitonia.placementRecommended: Bathroom with indirect natural light — high humidity and filtered light are perfect.
placementAlternatives: Terrarium or open glass tank
Kitchen table with indirect light
Next to a humidifier
fitonia.placementAvoid: Direct sun (scorches small leaves) and dark corners (loses vein pattern).
fitonia.whyRationale: Amazon understory — its white veins reflect the scarce light that reaches the forest floor. Thin leaves lose water fast, so it needs high humidity and steady watering.

ficus-lyrata.name: Fiddle Leaf Fig
ficus-lyrata.tip: Huge fiddle-shaped leaves. Once in its place, don't move it — it hates change.
ficus-lyrata.description: Fiddle Leaf Fig (Ficus lyrata) is an indoor tree from West Africa. Grows several meters with large, leathery leaves. Sensitive to movement and drafts — it complains with massive leaf drop. Stability is key.

problems.cause: Location change, draft, or watering stress
problems.solution: Keep a fixed spot, no drafts; water consistently — neither too much nor too little.
Use water rested for 24h; keep a steady watering rhythm.
Wipe the large leaves with a damp cloth every 2 weeks — dust blocks photosynthesis.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 4 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm: water until it drains; never let it go fully dry.

ficus-lyrata.placementRecommended: Near a large window with bright indirect light — filtered morning sun is ideal.
placementAlternatives: 1 m from an east window
Bright room with sheer curtain
Covered porch with filtered sun
ficus-lyrata.placementAvoid: Drafts (door, AC) and frequent location changes — drops leaves from stress.
ficus-lyrata.whyRationale: Native to West African tropical rainforest — grows as a hemi-epiphyte before rooting. Its large fiddle-shaped leaves capture filtered canopy light and are sensitive to environmental stress.

maranta.name: Prayer Plant
maranta.tip: Called prayer plant: folds leaves at dusk. High humidity and chlorine-free water.
maranta.description: Prayer Plant (Maranta leuconeura) has oval leaves with red or purple veins on light green. Native to Brazilian rainforests. It folds its leaves at dusk (nyctinasty) and unfolds them by day — a visible movement.

problems.solution: Use rested or rainwater; mist the air or run a humidifier.
Check medium indirect light and steady watering — nightly folding indicates health.
Move away from the window — maranta prefers medium indirect light.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water as soon as dry; never let it fully dry out.

maranta.placementRecommended: Room with medium indirect light — bathroom or kitchen with high natural humidity.
placementAlternatives: 2 m from a window
Room with a humidifier
Interior shelf away from windows
maranta.placementAvoid: Direct sun (fades color) and dry air — maranta doesn't tolerate dry environments.
maranta.whyRationale: Marantaceae from Brazilian rainforest — its nyctinasty (nightly folding via petiole pulvini) reduces water loss and predator exposure. Vein patterns reflect filtered understory light.

zamioculca.name: ZZ Plant
zamioculca.tip: Nearly indestructible. The most common mistake is overwatering.
zamioculca.description: ZZ Plant is a resilient species with succulent stems and water-storing rhizomes. Native to semi-arid East Africa. Tolerates low light and missed waterings — the best plant for beginners and dark offices.

problems.solution: Let the soil dry well between waterings.
Unpot, cut away soft tissue, and replant in dry substrate.
Reduce watering to minimum; new growth resumes in spring.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm: water only if completely dry. Less is more.

zamioculca.placementRecommended: Any corner with indirect light — even low-light spots.
placementAlternatives: Hallway or corner without a nearby window
Office with fluorescent lighting
Bedroom with low natural light
zamioculca.placementAvoid: Constantly damp soil and pots without drainage — rhizome rot is irreversible.
zamioculca.whyRationale: Native to semi-arid East Africa — its rhizomes store water and nutrients. Its CAM metabolism enables low-light and drought tolerance, but rot from overwatering is irreversible.

cola-burro.name: Donkey Tail
cola-burro.tip: Fragile leaves that fall off at the slightest touch. Place where no one will brush past.
cola-burro.description: Donkey Tail (Sedum morganianum) is a trailing succulent with stems covered in plump blue-green leaves. Native to Mexico. Its leaves drop at any contact — and each one can root in dry substrate.

problems.solution: Place out of traffic; each fallen leaf can be planted to propagate.
Reduce watering drastically; cut soft sections and replant in well-draining substrate.
Water slowly with small amounts several times until the succulent rehydrates.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil down to the drainage hole: if completely dry, water sparingly. It survives drought better than excess.

cola-burro.placementRecommended: Hanging pot in a bright but out-of-traffic spot — near a south-facing window with a sheer curtain.
placementAlternatives: High shelf with filtered sun
Hanging basket on a warm covered patio
Bright shelf away from people
cola-burro.placementAvoid: High-traffic areas (loses leaves) and constantly damp soil (stem rot).
cola-burro.whyRationale: Mexican succulent from rocky habitats — stores water in fleshy leaves and uses CAM metabolism to minimize daytime water loss. The fragile leaves enable vegetative propagation by detachment.

hiedra.name: English Ivy
hiedra.tip: Versatile climber. Variegated varieties need more light to keep their color.
hiedra.description: English Ivy (Hedera helix) is a European climber with lobed green or variegated leaves. Adaptable to cool interiors or sheltered outdoors. Grows in hanging baskets, on supports, or as a groundcover. Tolerates more cold than most houseplants.

problems.solution: Move to bright indirect light — cream and gold tones require more light.
Mist the air or group with other plants; lower the temperature if you can.
Shower the plant with lukewarm water; increase ambient humidity.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 4 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water when dry; less in winter.

hiedra.placementRecommended: Cool room with bright indirect light — variegated varieties near an east-facing window.
placementAlternatives: Hanging basket in a bright room
On a vertical support in a hallway
Cool covered porch
hiedra.placementAvoid: Direct heater air (dries leaves) and prolonged direct sun.
hiedra.whyRationale: European climber from temperate forests — its adventitious rootlets cling to surfaces to climb and share light. Tolerates cold to 5°C because its origin is temperate, not tropical.

palmera-areca.name: Areca Palm
palmera-areca.tip: Long arching leaves. Needs bright indirect light and steadily moist substrate.
palmera-areca.description: Areca Palm (Dypsis lutescens) is a tropical palm from Madagascar with light-green pinnate leaves and multiple stems. Grows several meters indoors in bright light. Sensitive to drought and also to waterlogging — constant balance.

problems.solution: Use water rested for 24h; mist the air or run a humidifier.
Improve drainage; touch the soil before watering — top should be barely dry.
Move near a large window with bright indirect light.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 4 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water when dry; keep steady moisture without waterlogging.

palmera-areca.placementRecommended: Near a large window with bright indirect light — stable warm temperature.
placementAlternatives: Bright living room with sheer curtain
Warm covered porch
1 m from a filtered south window
palmera-areca.placementAvoid: Midday direct sun (scorches fronds) and temperatures below 12°C.
palmera-areca.whyRationale: Palm from Madagascar — Arecaceae adapted to tropical understory. Tolerates dispersed light but requires fast drainage and steady humidity; its roots can't tolerate prolonged waterlogging.

palmera-kentia.name: Kentia Palm
palmera-kentia.tip: More shade-tolerant than the areca. Slow growth — patience is key.
palmera-kentia.description: Kentia Palm (Howea forsteriana) is an elegant palm from Lord Howe Island, Australia. Dark-green pinnate leaves and very slow growth. More tolerant of low-light interiors than other palms — ideal for hotels and offices.

problems.solution: Accept the pace: 1-2 new fronds per year. Patience.

problems.cause: Dry air or salt in water
problems.solution: Mist the air; use rested or rainwater.
Cut yellow fronds at the base — the plant remains vigorous.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 6 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm: water when dry; reduce greatly in winter.

palmera-kentia.placementRecommended: Living room with medium to bright indirect light — tolerates even deep interiors better than the areca.
placementAlternatives: Bright hallway
Hotel/office with mixed natural and artificial light
Spacious room away from windows
palmera-kentia.placementAvoid: Prolonged direct sun (scorches fronds) and overwatering (root rot).
palmera-kentia.whyRationale: Palm from Lord Howe (Australia) — endemic to shaded forests. Its low-light tolerance comes from understory adaptation, making it exceptional as a deep-interior palm.

helecho-boston.name: Boston Fern
helecho-boston.tip: Needs high ambient humidity. If you can't sustain it, choose another plant.
helecho-boston.description: Boston Fern (Nephrolepis exaltata) is an elegant fern with long, arching fronds. Native to American tropical forests. Demanding for ambient humidity — below 50% it starts losing fronds. Ideal for bright bathrooms.

problems.solution: Move to a bathroom or run a continuous humidifier; mist the fronds daily.
Keep soil barely damp — the fern doesn't tolerate drying out.
Stabilize location and humidity; new growth returns in weeks.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water as soon as dry. Soil always barely damp.

helecho-boston.placementRecommended: Bright bathroom with high natural humidity — medium indirect light.
placementAlternatives: Kitchen with natural light and cooking humidity
Room with a continuous humidifier
Hanging basket in a humid spot
helecho-boston.placementAvoid: Dry heater air (crispy fronds) and direct sun (scorches).
helecho-boston.whyRationale: American tropical fern — its sporophyte needs ambient humidity above 60% to maintain leaf turgor. Without it the fronds dry out irreversibly because the plant stores no water.

helecho-nido.name: Bird's Nest Fern
helecho-nido.tip: Entire wavy fronds — don't mist them, water only the substrate. More tolerant than Boston fern.
helecho-nido.description: Bird's Nest Fern (Asplenium nidus) has entire (undivided) fronds that grow forming a central nest. Native to Asian rainforests. Easier than Boston fern because it tolerates dry air better — but it still needs medium-high humidity and indirect light.

problems.cause: Water pooled in the central nest
problems.solution: Water the substrate only — never the rosette center.
Move away from the window — medium indirect light is ideal.
Mist the surrounding air (not the fronds) or group with other plants.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water the substrate (not the center) when dry.

helecho-nido.placementRecommended: Room with medium indirect light and high ambient humidity — bathroom works great.
placementAlternatives: Bright room with a humidifier
2 m from a north-facing window
Kitchen with natural light
helecho-nido.placementAvoid: Direct sun (scorches fronds) and water in the central nest (rot).
helecho-nido.whyRationale: Asian epiphytic fern — in habitat it grows in tree forks, collecting rainwater and debris in its nest. Its entire fronds evolved for that water-harvesting role.

pilea.name: Chinese Money Plant
pilea.tip: Round coin-like leaves. Produces shareable pups — the friendship plant.
pilea.description: Pilea peperomioides is native to southern China. Its round, flat leaves perch on slender stems. It produces baby shoots at the base that you can separate and gift — hence its names money plant and friendship plant.

problems.solution: Touch the soil: water only when the top 2 cm are dry.
Keep a stable location with bright indirect light.
Rotate the pot weekly for even growth.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 4 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water when dry. Rotate the pot weekly for symmetric form.

pilea.placementRecommended: Near a window with bright indirect light — rotate the pot weekly.
placementAlternatives: Table near an east-facing window
Bright shelf with sheer curtain
Kitchen with natural light
pilea.placementAvoid: Direct sun (scorches the round leaves) and dark corners (long, weak stem).
pilea.whyRationale: Native to Yunnan province, China — a temperate mountain habitat. Its round leaves are shields for bright indirect light. The rhizomatous pups enable easy clonal propagation.

tradescantia.name: Wandering Dude
tradescantia.tip: Grows fast. Cuttings root in water in 2 weeks — propagation is effortless.
tradescantia.description: Tradescantia zebrina is a Mexican trailing plant with leaves striped in purple, silver, and green. Fast growth, super-easy propagation from cuttings. Ideal in a hanging basket or on a high shelf where its stems can drape down.

problems.solution: Move to bright indirect light — purple requires light to express.
Prune stems every 1-2 months; use the cuttings to root and refill the pot.
Water generously — recovers fast but doesn't tolerate repeated drought.

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water when dry; keep a steady rhythm.

tradescantia.placementRecommended: Hanging basket or high shelf near a window with bright indirect light.
placementAlternatives: Hanging pot on a warm covered patio
High shelf in a bright room
Ledge with filtered morning sun
tradescantia.placementAvoid: Midday direct sun (fades color) and dark corners (loses purple).
tradescantia.whyRationale: Native to tropical Mexico — its anthocyanins in purple stripes need bright light to express. Fast vegetative propagation is an adaptation to disturbed environments.

cheflera.name: Dwarf Umbrella Tree
cheflera.tip: Umbrella-shaped leaves. Tolerant and fast-growing — good for beginners.
cheflera.description: Dwarf Umbrella Tree (Heptapleurum arboricola, formerly Schefflera) has digitate leaves that look like umbrellas. Native to Taiwan and Hainan. Tolerates low light but grows faster in bright light. Excellent starter plant for homes and offices.

problems.solution: Move closer to bright light; touch the soil before watering.
Wipe leaves with soapy water; inspect undersides weekly.
Move to bright indirect light; prune the top to force branching.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 4 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water when dry. Reduce greatly in winter.

cheflera.placementRecommended: Spot with bright indirect light — tolerates light variations better than most tropicals.
placementAlternatives: Living room with mixed natural light
Bright hallway
Office with mixed natural and artificial light
cheflera.placementAvoid: Prolonged direct sun (scorches variegation) and temperatures below 12°C.
cheflera.whyRationale: Formerly Schefflera arboricola — reclassified to Heptapleurum in 2024 (POWO). Native to Taiwan and Hainan, its tolerance to variable light comes from its origin in transitional tropical-temperate forests.

arbol-dinero.name: Money Tree
arbol-dinero.tip: Braided trunk and digitate leaves. Tolerant with watering — less is more.
arbol-dinero.description: Money Tree (Pachira aquatica) is native to Central American wetlands. Often sold with a decoratively braided trunk. Glossy green digitate leaves. Resilient and tolerant — its name comes from the popular belief that it attracts prosperity.

problems.solution: Reduce watering; touch the soil before — top should be quite dry.
Move to indirect light and mist the surrounding air.
Water generously and keep steady moisture for 2 weeks.

nutrients.homemade: Compost tea every 4 weeks in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm: water when dry. Its trunk stores water — less is more.

arbol-dinero.placementRecommended: Living room with bright indirect light or filtered sun — medium-high humidity helps but isn't essential.
placementAlternatives: 1 m from a filtered east or south window
Room with mixed light
Bright hallway
arbol-dinero.placementAvoid: Constantly damp soil (root rot) and midday direct sun (leaf scorch).
arbol-dinero.whyRationale: Native to Central American wetlands — its swollen trunk stores water to tolerate seasonal drought. Its tolerance to missed waterings comes from that adaptation to cyclic flooding-drought.

nopal.name: Prickly Pear (Nopal)
nopal.tip: Heavy, infrequent watering; full sun; tolerates light frosts.
nopal.description: The Prickly Pear (Opuntia ficus-indica) is a shrubby cactus native to Mexico with fleshy pads (cladodes) that store water. It produces yellow flowers and the well-known edible fruits called tunas. Lives outdoors or in very large pots; perfect for arid climates and sunny patios.

problems.solution: Drastically reduce watering and improve drainage (mix with perlite and coarse sand).
Clean with a brush and 70% isopropyl alcohol on affected areas; treat with potassium soap.
Cover with frost cloth on freezing nights, or move it under cover if potted.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted worm compost every 3 months in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 14 days in warm season; every 30 days in cold. Let dry completely between waterings.

nopal.placementRecommended: Full outdoor sun — patio, terrace, balcony with direct midday sun.
placementAlternatives: Large pot with generous drainage
Bed in sandy or gravelly soil
Wall facing north (SH) or south (NH) with full sun all day
nopal.placementAvoid: Waterlogged substrate and prolonged temperatures below 5°C.
nopal.whyRationale: Cactus from Mexican arid lands — CAM metabolism closes stomata by day and fixes CO2 at night; its pads are modified stems that store water. Nocturnal photosynthesis is the key to its desert tolerance.

mammillaria.name: Ladyfinger Cactus (Mammillaria)
mammillaria.tip: Ladyfinger cactus: full sun and spaced watering; flowers in an apical crown in spring.
mammillaria.description: Mammillaria elongata (ladyfinger cactus) is a clumping Mexican cactus: its cylindrical stems covered in golden spines form dense colonies. It blooms in a crown at the top as days lengthen. Ideal for sunny indoor shelves.

problems.solution: Move closer to a window with direct morning sun for at least 4-6 hours.
Let dry completely between waterings; use cactus substrate with perlite and coarse sand.
Apply 70% alcohol with a swab on affected areas and ventilate the plant.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted compost tea every 2 months in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water deeply every 14 days in warm season; every 30 days in cold. Let dry completely between waterings.

mammillaria.placementRecommended: Near a south window (NH) or north window (SH) with direct morning sun.
placementAlternatives: Bright shelf with filtered midday sun
Kitchen counter with good natural light
Covered balcony with direct morning sun
mammillaria.placementAvoid: Corners without direct light and damp substrate for more than 7 days in a row.
mammillaria.whyRationale: Mexican clumping cactus — its tubercles store water and its spines reflect light to reduce transpiration. Blooms in an apical crown as days lengthen: photoperiod triggers spring flowering.

cactus-navidad.name: Christmas Cactus
cactus-navidad.tip: Reduce watering in autumn to induce flowering; needs long nights (>13h darkness).
cactus-navidad.description: Christmas Cactus (Schlumbergera × buckleyi) is NOT a desert cactus: it's an epiphyte from Brazilian forests that grows on trees in partial shade. Its segmented stems are photosynthetic and it blooms in December-January if given the long-night cue in autumn.

problems.solution: Don't move it during flowering; keep environment stable between 15-22°C.
Let the top layer dry between waterings; use airy substrate with bark.
In autumn, give it 13-14h of nightly darkness for 6 weeks and reduce watering.

nutrients.type: Low nitrogen; high phosphorus and potassium (flowering)
nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea every 3 weeks in autumn-winter

careAction.fixed: Water every 7 days in warm season; every 14 in cold. Reduce in autumn to induce flowering.

cactus-navidad.placementRecommended: Near an east window — bright indirect light, soft mornings.
placementAlternatives: High shelf where its stems can hang
Bright room with filtering curtain
Covered porch without direct sun
cactus-navidad.placementAvoid: Direct midday sun (burns the segments) and temperatures below 10°C.
cactus-navidad.whyRationale: Not a desert cactus: an epiphyte from Brazilian forest — grows on trees in partial shade. Its segmented stems are photosynthetic and it blooms with long nights (>13h darkness) in autumn.

kalanchoe.name: Kalanchoe
kalanchoe.tip: Blooms with short days. For flowers, give it 14 hours of darkness per night.
kalanchoe.description: Kalanchoe blossfeldiana is a Madagascan crassulaceous succulent with toothed fleshy leaves and showy pink, red, orange, or white flower clusters. Tolerates missed waterings but needs prolonged nightly darkness to flower again.

problems.solution: Cover for 14h per night for 6 weeks to induce flowering.
Let the substrate dry out; its rhizomes rot quickly with constant moisture.
Move closer to a window with bright indirect sun.

nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea every 3 weeks during flowering

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm season; every 21 days in cold. Let dry well between waterings.

kalanchoe.placementRecommended: Window with filtered sun or bright indirect light.
placementAlternatives: Shelf near south window (SH) with translucent curtain
Covered balcony without direct midday sun
Kitchen counter with good natural light
kalanchoe.placementAvoid: Constantly moist substrate and low light.
kalanchoe.whyRationale: Madagascan crassulaceous succulent with CAM photosynthesis: closes stomata by day, fixes CO2 at night. Blooms with long nights (>14h darkness) — its photoperiodic clock needs that contrast to set buds.

siempreviva.name: Common Houseleek
siempreviva.tip: Tolerates extreme cold; after flowering, the mother rosette dies but leaves many offsets.
siempreviva.description: Common Houseleek (Sempervivum tectorum) is a crassulaceous from the European Alps and Pyrenees with dense rosettes that produce offsets around the mother. It's monocarpic: the matrix flowers once and dies, but its offspring continue the colony. Withstands frosts down to -10°C.

problems.solution: Move to a spot with full sun or bright indirect light at least 6h daily.
Let dry between waterings and use a clay pot with full drainage.
It's not death: cut the dry stalk; the offsets around it will continue.

nutrients.homemade: Diluted compost tea every 4 months in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water every 14 days in warm season; every 30 in cold. Let dry completely between waterings.

siempreviva.placementRecommended: Full sun or bright indirect — tolerates frosts down to -10°C outdoors.
placementAlternatives: Clay pot with full drainage
Rocky bed in full sun
Bright shelf facing south (NH) or north (SH)
siempreviva.placementAvoid: Waterlogged substrate and corners without direct light.
siempreviva.whyRationale: European mountain crassulaceous — its rosette habit retains water and minimizes cold-exposed surface. It's monocarpic: the mother flowers once and dies, but leaves dozens of offsets that replace her.

gasteria.name: Lawyer's Tongue (Gasteria)
gasteria.tip: Lawyer's tongue: tolerates more shade than a cactus; ideal for bright desks.
gasteria.description: Gasteria bicolor is a South African asphodelaceous succulent related to Aloe; its fleshy leaves are arranged in two rows (distichous) and are mottled. Unlike most succulents, it tolerates bright indirect light — a rare exception that makes it ideal for less-sunny interiors.

problems.solution: Move to bright indirect light; tolerates less sun than most cactaceae.
Let dry completely between waterings; use succulent substrate with perlite.
Clean with a swab and 70% alcohol; check between the distichous leaves.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted compost tea every 3 months in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water every 14 days in warm season; every 30 in cold. Let dry completely between waterings.

gasteria.placementRecommended: Bright indirect light — more shade than a typical cactus.
placementAlternatives: Shelf near an east-facing window
Counter with light filtered through a curtain
Bright office shelf or hallway
gasteria.placementAvoid: Direct midday sun and waterlogged substrate.
gasteria.whyRationale: South African asphodelaceous succulent of arid understories — distichous (two-row) leaves store water and tolerate partial shade. Its modified CAM lets it thrive where a full-sun cactus could not.

piedras-vivas.name: Living Stones (Lithops)
piedras-vivas.tip: Almost never water: absolute summer dormancy; touch the soil only in autumn-winter.
piedras-vivas.description: Living Stones (Lithops lesliei) are South African Aizoaceae mesembs that mimic stones in their habitat. They have an annual leaf-replacement cycle: the new pair absorbs water from the old pair. Absolute summer dormancy — a single warm-season watering can split them open.

problems.solution: Suspend watering completely from November to March (SH); old leaves should absorb on their own.
In autumn-winter water every 30 days after the soil is completely dry.
Ventilate the pot and keep substrate very mineral; repot if rot has progressed.

nutrients.homemade: Not applicable — mineral substrate is enough

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the soil only in autumn-winter; keep completely dry all summer (dormancy).

piedras-vivas.placementRecommended: Direct morning sun — east-facing window, covered balcony with morning sun.
placementAlternatives: Shallow pot with full drainage
Shelf facing south (NH) with translucent curtain
Kitchen counter with direct light
piedras-vivas.placementAvoid: Watering in midsummer (absolute dormancy) and prolonged humid air.
piedras-vivas.whyRationale: South African Aizoaceae mesemb — annual leaf-replacement cycle: the new pair absorbs water from the old pair. Absolute summer dormancy — a single summer watering can split it open as old leaves rehydrate and burst.

senecio-rowleyanus.name: String of Pearls (Curio rowleyanus)
senecio-rowleyanus.tip: Hanging basket with bright indirect light; reclassified as Curio (formerly Senecio).
senecio-rowleyanus.description: String of Pearls (Curio rowleyanus, formerly Senecio rowleyanus) is a South African Asteraceae succulent with spherical leaves that look like pearls. They store water and limit evaporative surface. Trailing habit ideal for hanging baskets; tolerates forgetfulness but hates waterlogging.

problems.solution: Water deeply when pearls look soft; they recover turgor in 2-3 days.
Let dry completely between waterings; check that the substrate drains well.
Move away from cold windows and doors in winter; keep above 12°C.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted compost tea every 3 months in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm season; every 21 in cold. Let dry well between waterings.

senecio-rowleyanus.placementRecommended: Hanging basket with bright indirect light — near an east-facing window.
placementAlternatives: High shelf where pearls can drape
Bright shelf with filtering curtain
Hanging planter in a lit room
senecio-rowleyanus.placementAvoid: Direct midday sun (burns pearls) and cold drafts below 10°C.
senecio-rowleyanus.whyRationale: South African Asteraceae succulent — its spherical leaves (pearls) store water and limit evaporative surface. Grows under arid canopy: tolerates forgetfulness but hates waterlogging.

corona-espinas.name: Crown of Thorns
corona-espinas.tip: Toxic when cut (white latex) — wear gloves when pruning; near-continuous flowering with sun.
corona-espinas.description: Crown of Thorns (Euphorbia milii) is a Madagascan thorny spurge with a cactus-like appearance — but it's NOT Cactaceae: it's evolutionary convergence. Its small, showy bracts (pink, red, yellow) replace petals. Its white sap is toxic when cut.

problems.solution: Move it closer to a very bright window; it tolerates and prefers direct sun.
Let dry completely between waterings; use very well-draining substrate.
Always wear gloves; on skin contact, wash with plenty of soap. Don't cut with children nearby.

nutrients.homemade: Banana peel tea every 4 weeks in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water every 10 days in warm season; every 21 in cold. Wear gloves when handling or pruning.

corona-espinas.placementRecommended: Full sun or direct light — south window (NH) or north (SH) with plenty of sun.
placementAlternatives: Covered balcony in direct sun
Clay pot with generous drainage
Bright shelf with morning and mid-afternoon sun
corona-espinas.placementAvoid: Dark corners (no flowering) and waterlogged substrate.
corona-espinas.whyRationale: Madagascan thorny spurge — its cactus-like appearance is evolutionary convergence, not true Cactaceae. White milky sap is toxic when cut. Near-continuous bloom in direct light because its bracts replace petals.

agave.name: Century Plant (Agave)
agave.tip: One-time monocarpic flowering after decades — the mother rosette dies after but leaves offsets.
agave.description: Agave americana is a Mexican Asparagaceae monocot with a giant rosette of fleshy leaves bearing marginal spines and a terminal spine. Combines CAM photosynthesis with water storage in leaves. It's monocarpic: after decades it sends up a giant stalk, blooms, seeds, and dies.

problems.symptom: Rot in the center of the rosette
problems.solution: Drastically reduce watering; improve drainage with coarse sand and perlite.

problems.symptom: Scale at the base of leaves
problems.solution: Clean with brush and soapy water; treat with potassium soap if it persists.
Use thick gloves; cut the terminal spine if it ends up in a walking area.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted worm compost once a year in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water every 21 days in warm season; every 60 in cold. Let dry completely between waterings.

agave.placementRecommended: Full outdoor sun — patio, terrace, garden.
placementAlternatives: Large pot with full drainage
Rocky bed in full sun
South wall (NH) with direct sun all day
agave.placementAvoid: Waterlogged substrate and corners without full sun.
agave.whyRationale: Xerophytic rosette succulent — CAM photosynthesis + water storage in fleshy leaves. One-time monocarpic flowering after decades: the mother sends up a giant stalk, blooms, seeds, and dies — but leaves offsets behind.

hoya.name: Sweetheart Plant (Hoya kerrii)
hoya.tip: Very slow grower. Forgiving, loves high humidity.
hoya.description: Hoya kerrii (also called heart plant or Sweetheart Plant) is a Southeast Asian asclepiadaceous succulent vine with thick heart-shaped leaves. Often sold as a single-leaf gift; given time, it can develop long climbing or trailing tendrils. It is very slow — patience required.

problems.solution: Patience: 2-3 new leaves per year is normal.
Water thoroughly and wait days for rehydration; its leaves store water.
Reduce watering; check drainage; move away from cold drafts.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted compost tea every 2 months in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm: if dry, water; in cold reduce to almost nothing.

hoya.placementRecommended: Hanging basket with bright indirect light — near an east-facing or north-facing window (SH).
placementAlternatives: High shelf where its tendrils can hang
Bright shelf with high ambient humidity
Pot with a support to climb
hoya.placementAvoid: Direct sun (burns its fleshy leaves) and prolonged dry heater air.
hoya.whyRationale: Climbing Southeast Asian asclepiadaceous succulent — heart-shaped leaves with modified CAM tissue. Tolerates forgetfulness but grows very slowly: years of patience are built into its natural rhythm.

mini-monstera.name: Mini Monstera (Rhaphidophora)
mini-monstera.tip: Needs a support to fenestrate. Without a pole, stays juvenile and hole-less.
mini-monstera.description: Rhaphidophora tetrasperma is a hemi-epiphytic climbing aroid from Malaysia and Thailand, often confused and mis-sold as Monstera minima, Philodendron Ginny, or Philodendron Piccolo (none of which are valid Latin names). It develops fenestrations (heart-shaped holes) ONLY when it matures climbing a support; without a moss pole it stays in juvenile small-leaf form.

problems.cause: Lack of vertical support to climb
problems.solution: Add a moss pole or stake so the plant can mature and develop fenestrations.
Reduce frequency; check drainage; let the top centimeters dry out.
Guide them toward the moss pole or mist them to keep them hydrated.

nutrients.homemade: Diluted compost tea once a month in warm season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 2 cm: water until it drains.

mini-monstera.placementRecommended: Near a window with bright indirect light + a moss pole if you want big leaves with holes.
placementAlternatives: Bright room with humidifier
Hanging basket where it can climb a wall
High shelf with a vertical support
mini-monstera.placementAvoid: Direct sun and dark corners (no support, no fenestrations).
mini-monstera.whyRationale: Climbing hemi-epiphytic aroid from Southeast Asia — fenestrations appear once it reaches vertical support (heteroblasty). Without a pole it stays juvenile without holes. Hence confusion with Monstera, but it's Rhaphidophora.

strelitzia.name: Bird of Paradise (Strelitzia)
strelitzia.tip: Flowers after 3-4 years. Needs lots of sun and maturity.
strelitzia.description: Strelitzia reginae compact (~1-1.5m) is a South African strelitziaceous species known as Bird of Paradise for its iconic flower (orange, blue, and cream sepals shaped like a tropical bird). It has large paddle-shaped leaves; needs YEARS of bright indirect light to flower indoors. Do NOT confuse with the giant S. nicolai (2-3m, white flowers — a different species not covered by this entry).

problems.solution: It's normal — not damage; the leaf splits along veins to resist gusts.
Move closer to a large window with morning sun; wait 3-4 years from seed.

problems.cause: Low ambient humidity or dry air
problems.solution: Mist the leaves; move away from heaters; group with other plants.

nutrients.homemade: Diluted compost tea every 4 weeks during the growing season

careAction.soilCheck: Touch the top 3 cm: water when dry. Reduce drastically in winter.

strelitzia.placementRecommended: Near a large window with bright indirect light — direct morning sun helps it flower.
placementAlternatives: Bright room with sheer curtain on midday direct sun
East-facing veranda with many hours of light
Sheltered outdoor bed in temperate climate (SH)
strelitzia.placementAvoid: Dark corners (won't flower) and prolonged temperatures below 8°C.
strelitzia.whyRationale: South African strelitziaceous savanna species — paddle-shaped leaves reduce water loss. Flowering needs prolonged bright light + 3-4 years of maturity. The bird-beak-shaped sepal evolved for sunbird pollination.

eucalipto.name: Lemon Eucalyptus
eucalipto.tip: Strong citrus aroma from citronellal. Use leaves for infusions; full outdoor sun.
eucalipto.description: Eucalyptus citriodora (POWO synonym Corymbia citriodora since the 2000s) is an Australian myrtaceous tree with intensely citrus-scented leaves due to the volatile compound citronellal (anti-herbivory defense and natural insect repellent). Very fast outdoor growth (>1m per year); difficult to maintain in a pot long-term — better planted in ground. The Argentine market mostly sells it under the Eucalyptus name.

problems.solution: Transplant to an extra-large pot or plant directly in the ground if possible.
Apply chelated iron amendment; acidify substrate with peat.
Wipe with cotton swab and alcohol; treat with potassium soap if it persists.

nutrients.homemade: Compost at the base in spring; iron amendment if leaves yellow

careAction.fixed: Water every 5 days in warm season thoroughly; every 10 in cold. Fast growth.

eucalipto.placementRecommended: Full outdoor sun — patio, garden, large terrace with deep substrate.
placementAlternatives: Bed in well-drained sandy-clay soil
Extra-large pot (50+ liters) with full drainage
North-facing wall (SH) with all-day sun
eucalipto.placementAvoid: Small pots (invasive roots) and waterlogged soil.
eucalipto.whyRationale: Australian myrtaceous tree — leaves carry volatile citronellal (anti-herbivory defense). Very fast outdoor growth: the tree races for height, which is why long-term pot confinement is hard.

bambu-suerte.name: Lucky Bamboo
bambu-suerte.tip: Grows in either water or soil. In water, change every 10-14 days with filtered water.
bambu-suerte.description: Dracaena sanderiana (NOT a true bamboo despite the name) is a West African asparagaceous, commonly sold as lucky bamboo in water culture (no substrate, only filtered water). It also grows in soil. Tolerates very low light. Sensitive to fluoride and chlorine in tap water — use filtered water or water settled 24h. The decorative spirals and braids are commercially shaped, not natural.

problems.cause: Fluoride or chlorine from tap water
problems.solution: Switch to filtered water or water settled 24h; fluoride accumulates in the tips.

problems.cause: Rot from old water in water culture
problems.solution: Change water every 10-14 days; rinse roots; trim rotted stems.
Mist leaves; move away from heaters; group with other plants.

nutrients.homemade: In water culture, a few drops of diluted liquid fertilizer once a month

careAction.fixed: If in water: change every 10-14 days with filtered water. If in soil: water every 14 days.

bambu-suerte.placementRecommended: Medium indirect light — desk, office shelf, counter with little natural light.
placementAlternatives: Room with fluorescent or LED light
Lit hallway with indirect light
Bright bathroom counter (high humidity)
bambu-suerte.placementAvoid: Direct sun (burns leaves) and unfiltered tap water with chlorine or fluoride.
bambu-suerte.whyRationale: African asparagaceous from riparian zones — adapted to high humidity, grows well in water culture. Sensitive to tap-water fluoride because its native habitat has soft water: fluoride accumulates in the tips and burns them.

sansevieria-cilindrica.name: Cylindrical Sansevieria
sansevieria-cilindrica.tip: Nearly indestructible. More tolerant than the common sansevieria.
sansevieria-cilindrica.description: Dracaena angolensis (POWO reclassified from Sansevieria cylindrica in 2018; the trade still uses the Sansevieria name) is an Angolan asparagaceous with cylindrical tubular leaves growing upright. Combines CAM photosynthesis with extreme drought tolerance. Tolerates very low light — one of the most forgiving houseplants. Often sold with leaves decoratively braided.

problems.cause: Overwatering — the most common cause of death
problems.solution: Reduce drastically; let dry completely; improve drainage with perlite.
Suspend watering for several days; repot if substrate stays wet.

problems.cause: Low light — normal in its nature
problems.solution: It's expected; if you want faster growth, move to bright indirect light.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted compost tea once or twice a year in warm season

careAction.fixed: Water every 21 days in warm season; every 45 in cold. Forget about it without guilt — it prefers drought.

sansevieria-cilindrica.placementRecommended: Bright indirect or medium light — versatile; tolerates office, hallway, bedroom.
placementAlternatives: Bright shelf with filtered light
Office corner with low natural light
Counter with indirect light
sansevieria-cilindrica.placementAvoid: Waterlogged substrate and prolonged temperatures below 8°C.
sansevieria-cilindrica.whyRationale: Angolan asparagaceous — cylindrical tubular leaves with water-storage tissue and CAM photosynthesis. Extreme tolerance to low light, drought, and neglect: in habitat it survives semi-arid savanna with little rain.

cactus-san-pedro.name: San Pedro Cactus
cactus-san-pedro.tip: Fast vertical growth. Full sun and minimal watering.
cactus-san-pedro.description: Echinopsis pachanoi (POWO reclassified from Trichocereus pachanoi in 2024; the trade still mostly uses Trichocereus) is a columnar cactus from the Andean foothills (Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, Chile). It has rapid vertical growth (>1cm per year), full outdoor sun, and develops impressive height (2-6m at maturity). Large, white, nocturnal flowers appear on mature specimens. This entry is purely horticultural — about plant care as a columnar ornamental.

problems.solution: Suspend watering; cut the rotten zone back to healthy tissue; let it callus before resuming.
Wipe with cotton swab and alcohol; treat with potassium soap if it persists.
Shelter from severe winter; move to a porch or cover if frost is forecast.

nutrients.homemade: Heavily diluted compost once a year at the start of warm season

careAction.fixed: Water every 14 days in warm season thoroughly; every 30 in cold. Let dry completely.

cactus-san-pedro.placementRecommended: Full outdoor sun — patio, terrace, garden with all-day direct sun.
placementAlternatives: Large pot with full drainage
Rocky bed in full sun
South-facing wall (NH) with direct sun
cactus-san-pedro.placementAvoid: Waterlogged substrate and indoor corners without full sun (stretching + weakness).
cactus-san-pedro.whyRationale: Andean foothills columnar cactus — CAM metabolism and rapid vertical growth (>1cm/year). Full outdoor sun, minimal watering. The column rises seeking direct light, characteristic of Echinopsis.
